questions.question: Compare and contrast the client-server and peer-to-peer architectural models for distributed systems. Discuss at least two key advantages and one significant disadvantage of each model.
keyPoints: Client-server model relies on dedicated servers and clients with specific roles, centralizing management and security.
Peer-to-peer model consists of equal peers that share resources and responsibilities, promoting decentralization and scalability.
Advantage of client-server: Easier to manage and secure. Disadvantage: Can be a single point of failure (the server).
Advantage of P2P: Highly scalable and fault-tolerant. Disadvantage: Can be more complex to secure and manage.

questions.question: Explain the role of middleware as an enabling technology in a distributed system. Describe two specific types of middleware and the problem each one is designed to solve.
keyPoints: Middleware is a software layer that provides a common programming abstraction and masks heterogeneity of underlying hardware, networks, and operating systems.
Remote Procedure Call (RPC) middleware: Solves the problem of making a procedure call on a remote network node appear as local to the programmer.
Message-Oriented Middleware (MOM): Solves the problem of asynchronous, reliable communication between distributed components using message queues.

questions.question: A large international bank is designing a new system for real-time fraud detection that must process millions of transactions per hour from global sources. Justify which distributed system model (e.g., client-server, three-tier, distributed objects) would be most suitable for this application. In your answer, explain two key technologies that would be critical to its implementation.
keyPoints: A multi-tier or distributed object model would be most suitable to handle the high volume, scalability, and modularity required.
Justification: Separates presentation, application logic, and data into distinct layers, allowing for load balancing and independent scaling of the processing tier.
Key Technology 1: A distributed messaging system (e.g., Apache Kafka) for reliably ingesting and buffering high-volume transaction streams.
Key Technology 2: A distributed in-memory data grid (e.g., Apache Ignite, Hazelcast) for storing and analyzing transaction patterns in real-time across multiple nodes.
